# Cross-model and cross-harness — final consolidated results

*Generated by research-copilot. This is the canonical, post-bug-fix
summary that supersedes the per-experiment notes from earlier today.*

## Headline panel

| Harness | Backbone | Vanilla | X+M | SE |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| CC | \texttt{deepseek-v4-flash} ($n=3$) | $0.910 \pm 0.024$ | $\mathbf{0.921 \pm 0.007}$ | $0.919 \pm 0.020$ |
| CC | \texttt{minimax-m2.7} ($n=1$) | $0.821$ ($1$ fail) | $\mathbf{0.867}$ ($0$ fail) | $0.861$ ($0$ fail) |
| CC | \texttt{google/gemini-3-flash-preview} ($n=1$) | $0.768$ ($0$ fail) | $\mathbf{0.797}$ ($0$ fail) | $0.757$ ($1$ fail) |
| OH | \texttt{deepseek-v4-flash} ($n=3$) | $0.856 \pm 0.061$ | $\mathbf{0.881 \pm 0.023}$ | n/a |

All cells under default-off harness (`GEOS_HOOK_POSTTOOLUSE` unset),
matching the autocamp-experiment-state harness. Failure counts are
out of $17$ tasks per seed.

## Three findings

### 1. X+M wins on every backbone we tested

The autocamp-best cell (X+M) outperforms Vanilla on all three
backbones: $+0.011$ on DSv4, $+0.046$ on minimax, $+0.029$ on gemini.
The lift size correlates inversely with backbone capability —
adapters help most where the baseline is weakest. SE is competitive
but not dominant: it leads on minimax ($0.861$ vs X+M $0.867$ within
single-seed noise) but loses to X+M on DSv4 (within seed noise) and
loses to Vanilla on gemini (a real anti-effect, see §3 below).

### 2. Cross-harness gap is consistent at $-4$ to $-5$ pp; X+M lift is bigger on the weaker harness

OH × DSv4 underperforms CC × DSv4 by $0.054$ at Vanilla and $0.040$
at X+M. The X+M adapter helps more on OH ($+0.025$) than on CC
($+0.011$) — same backbone, same cell content, different harness.
On both harnesses, X+M reduces seed $\sigma$ by ${\sim}3\times$
($0.024 \to 0.007$ on CC, $0.061 \to 0.023$ on OH).

### 3. SE on gemini regresses below Vanilla

Gemini × SE scored $0.757$ vs gemini × Vanilla's $0.768$ ($-0.011$).
Not seen on DSv4 or minimax. SE works on the other backbones because
its plugin includes both geos-rag and xmllint MCP servers, so the
agent has more tools to invoke; on gemini, the additional MCP surface
either confuses the agent or doesn't help. We do not investigate this
further at $n=1$; it is plausible that multi-seed would shrink the
effect.

## Bug discovered during cross-model

**TL;DR**: an internal user-prompt prefix told the agent to call MCP
tools that weren't registered. DSv4 ignored the impossible
instruction; minimax dutifully complied and emitted unroutable
pseudo-tool-calls, collapsing minimax × X+M to $0.392$ on the first
launch.

**Discovery path**:

1. First minimax × X+M run scored $0.392$ with $8/17$ tasks failing
   entirely (zero XMLs written).
2. Trajectory inspection showed minimax emitting text like
   `<minimax:tool_call><invoke name="mcp__geos-rag__search_navigator">...`
   in its first message, with zero real `tool_use` invocations.
3. We initially hypothesised "training-data prior" — minimax having
   memorised the geos-rag namespace from public agentic data.
4. The user pushed back: minimax-m2.7 (released 2026-03-18) predates
   our repo (first commit 2026-04-17). minimax cannot have been
   trained on our `mcp__geos-rag__*` tool names.
5. Adversarial review (RN-006) found the actual leakage path:
   `src/runner/prompts/native_plugin_prefix.txt` literally contains
   the string "Use the GEOS RAG MCP tools directly:
   `mcp__geos-rag__search_navigator`, `mcp__geos-rag__search_schema`,
   and `mcp__geos-rag__search_technical`. Before writing XML, call
   at least one of the plugin RAG tools."
6. The orchestrator at `src/runner/orchestrator.py:267` was injecting
   this prefix into the user prompt for every cell with
   `plugin_enabled=True`, regardless of whether the geos-rag MCP
   server was actually registered.
7. Fix: gate the prefix on `_rag_on` (whether geos-rag is actually
   registered), not on `enable_plugin`.

**Three-way comparison (minimax × X+M × test-17, $n=1$ seed)**:

| config | fa0 | scored | fail | pseudo MCP calls |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| X+M (no disclaim, buggy prefix) | $0.392$ | $9$ | $8$ | $18$ |
| X+M (disclaim, buggy prefix) | $0.711$ | $14$ | $3$ | $12$ |
| **X+M (prefix gate fixed, no disclaim)** | **$0.867$** | **$17$** | **$0$** | **$0$** |

The disclaimer fix (an in-prompt "do not call mcp\_\_geos-rag\_\_*"
warning) recovered $+31.9$pp by adding contradictory instructions to
the prompt. Removing the bad instruction at its source recovers an
additional $+15.6$pp on top of that, yields zero pseudo-calls, and
zero task failures.

## Spillover for the autocamp R main effect

The buggy prefix was active for cells with `plugin_enabled=True,
rag_enabled=False` and no `add_native_plugin_prefix=False` opt-out.
This includes:

- autocamp factorial: F2, F4, F6, F8, F11
- ablation cells: abl_c2, c5, c6, c7, c10, c11
- interactive-autonomy: ia_F4_*, ia_F0_interactive_*

Cells already opted out (unaffected): abl_se_round, abl_c9_no_prefix,
autocamp_SE, autocamp_v4. The `abl_c9_no_prefix` cell was created
specifically because the team had previously detected a $+0.24$
DSv4 anomaly attributable to this prefix — consistent with our
finding.

The reported autocamp $R = -0.033$ main effect therefore partially
measures "agent gets stuck on impossible instruction in $R^{-}$
cells" rather than a clean RAG-vs-no-RAG contrast. **A clean re-run
of F0/F4/F6/SE on DSv4 with the fixed gate is recommended for the
camera-ready.** Estimate: ${\sim}1.5$h wall-clock at 4 workers,
${\sim}\$0.50$ in DSv4 API spend.

The X+M-vs-Vanilla relative ranking is unaffected by this re-run
because both X+M and Vanilla are $R^{-}$ cells; the bug applied to
one of them only (X+M; Vanilla has `plugin_enabled=False` so the
prefix wasn't injected at all). So the X+M $> $ Vanilla ranking on
DSv4-flash test-17 ($+0.011$) is conservative against the bug.

## What this means for the paper

**The cross-cutting story is now clean and supportive of the X+M
recommendation**:

1. X+M generalises across the three backbones we tested.
2. X+M generalises across two harnesses (CC and OH).
3. The bug discovery is itself a useful methodological finding — it
   demonstrates that backbone choice can surface latent bugs in your
   harness because different models have different tolerance for
   "instructions that don't match available tools".

The paper's main-text cross-cutting subsection (§6.7) is now ${\sim}1$
paragraph; the full panel and bug-discovery details are in
App.~\ref{app:cross-model-detail}. The Limitations section (§7) flags
the $R$ main effect spillover and recommends the clean re-run for
camera-ready.
